text: Urgent, totally robotic right colectomy for sacral volvulus. This is an eighty-one year old female with abdominal pain and vomiting for two days, admitted with a sacral volvulus, BMI of thirty-eight. Past medical history and cervical history as stated above. CT scan demonstrating the sacral volvulus with diameter almost nine centimeters. This is a different view.

The surgical team docks the robotic arms in preparation for the urgent right colectomy. The table is set up with four ports, each 8.5 millimeters in size, with one port upsized for the stapled radial arm.

The surgeon targets the right upper quadrant, exposing the ileocecal area and identifying the distended cecum twisted due to sacral volvulus. The hepatic flexure is also visualized. Suction and irrigation are used to remove fluid from the pelvis, while a 4x4 gauze is employed for soft retraction.

The surgeon uses a purse-string suture to decompress the distended cecum, employing a 3-0 Vicryl suture. Gut suction is applied to decompress the colon, allowing for better manipulation of the bowel. The surgeon then begins the division and lateral mobilization of the ascending colon and cecum, using the fourth robotic arm for retraction and exposure. The mesentery appears redundant in this controlled surgical setting.

The surgeon carefully retracts and exposes the surgical field to prevent major spillage. A vessel sealer is used to divide the mesentery, marking the proximal division area of the distal ileum and creating a window. The division is performed close to the colon, near the hepatic flexure, as this is not a cancer operation.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection for the division of the mesentery, with the duodenum visible in the right lower corner of the frame. The distal margin is prepared for transection, utilizing the robotic fourth arm for optimal exposure and retraction without the need for laparoscopic trocars.

In the right upper quadrant, the surgeon completes the mobilization of the transverse colon. A 45 blue load stapler is used to divide the colon, followed by intravenous ICG administration for visualization. A suture is placed for retraction, and a colotomy is made with scissors, showing decompression of the colon. Another controlled enterotomy is performed using scissors, with robotic arms and instruments ensuring no spillage into the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon sutures the common channel using a 3-0 barbed suture in a running fashion, with the fourth robotic arm providing exposure for the anastomosis. The suture is initially tightened, but left slightly loose as the procedure progresses to ensure a proper closure. Indocyanine green (ICG) or Motan may be used towards the end to verify adequate blood supply to the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the closure of the anastomosis, locking the sutures in place. The colon is removed through the Alexis wound protector at the stapler port. A drain is placed next to the anastomosis for urgent cases, to be removed before discharge.
